full_response:
We work in some inertial frame S whose time-coordinate is t.  
In that frame the particle has  

3–velocity ⃗v(t) , |⃗v| = v, β ≡ v/c , γ ≡ 1/√(1-β²).

We are asked for the components of the 4-acceleration  

A^μ ≡ dU^μ/dτ,  U^μ = dx^μ/dτ = γ(c, ⃗v).

Assumptions and simplifications  
1. The ordinary (Newtonian) 3-acceleration ⃗a ≡ d⃗v/dt is everywhere parallel to ⃗v.  
2. We rotate the spatial axes so that the common direction of ⃗v and ⃗a is the +x-axis.  
   Thus ⃗v = v 𝑒_x , ⃗a = a 𝑒_x , and all y- and z-components vanish.

Step 1: relate coordinate-time and proper-time derivatives  
dt/dτ = γ  ⇒  d/dτ = γ d/dt .

Step 2: time component A⁰  

U⁰ = γ c  
A⁰ = dU⁰/dτ = γ d(γ c)/dt.

The derivative of γ with respect to t (for motion in one dimension) is  

dγ/dt = γ³ β a / c      (because γ = (1-β²)^{-1/2})

Hence  

A⁰ = γ c (γ³ β a / c) = γ⁴ β a
     = γ⁴ (v a)/c .                                       (1)

Step 3: spatial component Aˣ  

Uˣ = γ v  
Aˣ = γ d(γ v)/dt = γ [v(dγ/dt) + γ a].

Insert dγ/dt :

v dγ/dt = v (γ³ β a / c) = γ³ β² a .

Therefore  

Aˣ = γ (γ³ β² a + γ a)
    = γ⁴ β² a + γ² a
    = γ² a (1 + γ² β²).

Because γ² β² = γ²(1-1/γ²) = γ² – 1 ,

1 + γ² β² = γ² ,

so  

Aˣ = γ² a · γ² = γ⁴ a.                                  (2)

Step 4: y- and z-components  

Aʸ = Aᶻ = 0  (by the choice of axes).

Collecting the results (β = v/c):

A^μ = (A⁰ , Aˣ , Aʸ , Aᶻ)
     = ( γ⁴ β a , γ⁴ a , 0 , 0 ).

In 3-vector form, restoring the original direction of motion,

A^μ = ( γ⁴ (⃗v·⃗a)/c , γ⁴ ⃗a )            (⃗a ∥ ⃗v).

One may check that U_μ A^μ = 0, as required for any 4-acceleration.

\boxed{A^\mu =\left(\gamma^{4}\beta\,a,\; \gamma^{4}a,\;0,\;0\right)\quad\Bigl(\text{with } \beta=\dfrac{v}{c},\;a=\dfrac{dv}{dt}\Bigr)}

Confidence: 90%
